segments.s399-g57: کسی را که = a relative clause 'anyone who/whose...'; here را after کسی functions as the topical/object marker in a classical/poetic relative construction. "دختر بود آبکش" uses a reversed, poetic word order: the copula بود (past 'was') appears before the predicate noun آبکش (a colander/sieve), so literally 'the daughter was a sieve' but with the predicate after the verb for metrical/style reasons.
segments.s399-g58: This is two coordinated verbless nominal phrases giving the result/state: 'پسر در غم' = 'the son (is) in grief' (ellipsis of copula, common in poetry) joined by و to 'باب در خواب خوش' = 'باب (is) in sweet/pleasant sleep'. Note خواب خوش = 'pleasant sleep' where خوش postmodifies خواب. The noun باب here has no clear matching modern sense in the provided list (see senses).
segments.s399-g59: This is an optative/poetic formula: پدر آسمان باد = 'may the father be the sky' where باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)' rather than 'wind'. Word order places the predicate (آسمان) after the subject (پدر) and finishes with باد to mark the wish. و مادر زمین parallels the first half: 'and the mother (be) the earth.'
segments.s399-g60: نخوانم is first-person negative (poetic) meaning 'I do not / I will not utter.' Here خواندن is used in the sense 'to call out / to acclaim' and is negated. برین روزگار = بر این روزگار (orthographic ین = این) means 'upon these times' (بر = on). آفرین is an interjection 'bravo/praise' and completes the verb-object idea 'I will not call out “bravo” on these times.'
segments.s399-g61: پس از کلبه برخاست = 'then/from the cottage he rose'; پس here is a temporal/discourse 'then' (or 'after'), از = 'from', and برخاست is a past tense verb 'rose/left'. In classical/poetic word order the verb برخاست appears before its subject مرد جوان ('the young man').
segments.s399-g62: به نزدیک ارجاسپ آمد دوان = 'he came running to/near Arjasp.' به نزدیک = 'toward/near', ارجاسپ is the proper name, آمد is the past 'came', and دوان (literally 'running') is used adverbially/participially after آمد to give 'came running' (a common poetic collocation).
segments.s399-g63: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' where بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' and گفت is the past verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses do not list 'to say' so that item is marked accordingly in senses). کای is a vocative particle 'O', so کای شاه = 'O king', and فرخنده باش is an imperative/wish 'be fortunate' (باش = be!, imperative/optative).
segments.s399-g64: جهاندار تا جاودان زنده باش = an address plus optative wish: جهاندار 'sovereign/ruler' vocatively addressed; تا here expresses the desired duration ('until/so that' used idiomatically to mean 'that you may be... forever'), جاودان 'eternal', زنده 'alive', and باش is the imperative/optative 'be!'. The whole clause = 'Sovereign, may you live eternally.'
segments.s399-g9: شد is the past tense verb 'became' (here: his state changed). The prepositional phrase از کار ایشان ('from/of their deed') gives the cause: از is the poetic/archaic 'from/of', کار here means 'deed/affair', and ایشان is the plural pronoun 'they/their'. دلش is the noun 'his heart' with the clitic ش marking possession. پر ز بیم is an adjectival phrase 'full of fear' where پر = 'full (of)' takes ز (poetic 'of/because of') plus بیم 'fear'.
segments.s399-g10: بپوشید is a past narrative form meaning 'he covered/hid' (here transitive 'covered'). رخ را is the definite object 'the face' (را marks the direct object). به زیر گلیم is a prepositional phrase 'under the kilim' (به + زیر = 'into/under' + گلیم 'kilim/saddle‑cloth'), describing where he hid his face.
segments.s399-g11: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/advanced'. هر دو 'both' qualifies the subject. به نزدیک + اوی is a locative phrase 'to/near him' — اوی is an archaic/clitic form meaning 'him/it', attached after نزدیک; word order (verb ... adverbial) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s399-g12: ز خون برنهاده is an absolute/participial construction meaning 'having been (or with) blood placed/smeared' — ز is the poetic 'with/from', خون 'blood', and برنهاده (a composite verbal/adjectival form) 'placed/laid on' or 'smeared'. به رخ‌بر دو جوی is a verbal phrase 'their faces turned toward the two streams' where رخ‌بر is the verbalized compound 'to turn one's face' and جوی 'stream'; به indicates direction/toward.
segments.s399-g13: به خواهش is an adverbial phrase 'by/at the request' or 'upon entreaty' (به here marks the circumstance). گرفتند is the past plural 'they seized/took'. بیچارگان is the plural noun 'the helpless/the wretched' and functions here as the subject performing the action (word order is flexible in classical Persian).
segments.s399-g14: بران is a demonstrative/relational particle 'on that / that (one)/thereupon' pointing to the person taken. نامور is an adjective 'renowned/famed' modifying مرد 'man', and بازارگان specifies his social role 'merchant/tradesman' — together 'that famed merchant'. The phrase is the object of گرفتند.
segments.s399-g15: بدو is a prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او contracted). گفت here is the narrative verb 'said' (the listed noun senses do not include the verbal sense, see note). خواهر is the speaker 'the sister'. که‌ای ساروان is an archaic vocative/interrogative 'O (you) caravan‑leader' or 'who are you, caravan‑leader?' — که‌ای combines the interrogative/particle that introduces the direct question and ساروان is the vocative 'caravan‑leader/driver'.
segments.s399-g16: نخست is 'first/initially' modifying the manner of the action. از کجا is the interrogative 'from where'. راندی is second person singular past 'you drove' (classical past form of راندن), and کاروان is the object 'the caravan'; the clause asks 'from where did you first drive the caravan?'
segments.s399-g65: یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain' (used like 'a' or 'one') modifying the noun phrase that follows. ژرف دریا is the noun phrase 'deep sea' (ژرف = 'deep' before the noun دریا = 'sea'). درین راه is a locative phrase: در (in) + the dialectal/poetic ین (variant of این) making 'in this' (درین = 'in this/along this'), followed by راه ('route/path'). بود is the past copula 'was' agreeing with the whole clause.
segments.s399-g66: که introduces a relative clause describing the preceding sea. بازارگان is the subject 'the merchants'. زان (از + آن) refers back to 'that/it' (i.e. the sea). آگاه بود is the adjectival predicate 'were aware' — with the negation (نه) present in the original line it becomes 'were not aware of it' (the negation 'نه' is not listed among the segment words but negates آگاه). The verb بود is past tense 'were'.
segments.s399-g67: ز دریا is a prepositional phrase 'from the sea' (ز is the poetic form of از). برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'. یکی modifies the following noun phrase as 'a certain' or 'one'. کژ is an adjective 'crooked/askew' placed before باد ('wind'). The whole clause means 'a certain crooked wind rose from the sea.'
segments.s399-g68: که introduces reported speech or a clause referring to the wind. ملاح is 'the helmsman/sailor' (subject). گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'said' (introducing what follows). آن ندارم به یاد is direct speech: آن = 'that/it', ندارم = 'I do not have' (negated present: 'I do not have/recall'), and به یاد = 'in memory' → together 'I do not recall that.' Grammatically, گِفت + quoted clause/report is classical narrative word order.
segments.s399-g69: به کشتی is a locative phrase 'on/into the ship' (به + noun used to mark location). همه is the subject 'all of us'. زار و گریان شدیم is the compound predicate: زار 'wretched/lamenting' and گریان 'weeping', with شدیم 'we became' (past plural). The phrase describes the group's emotional state aboard the ship.
segments.s399-g70: ز marks source/cause 'from'. جان و تن خویش = 'our life and body' (جان = life/soul; تن = body). خویش is built from خوی + suffix ش (the list shows these split): the ش is the possessive element ('his/her/its' historically; here functioning as 'our/own' in context). بریان شدیم literally 'we became roasted/burnt' (بریان used metaphorically for extreme suffering). شدیم is past plural 'we became'.
segments.s399-g71: پذیرفتم is a first-person past verb 'I accepted/received/appealed to' (here best read as 'I turned to/implored'). از marks source 'from' or 'to' depending on verb valency; دادگر is 'the just one' (an epithet meaning just ruler or Just God) and یک خدای 'one God' serves as the object/appositive describing whom the speaker addressed. Word order places source/agent (از دادگر) before the noun phrase received/addressed.
segments.s399-g72: که‌گر (classical compound 'که گر') = 'if' (conditional). یابم is the present-subjunctive/optative first-person singular 'I may find/obtain' (expressing a hypothetical). از بیم دریا = 'from the fear/dread of the sea' (از = 'from', بیم = 'fear', دریا = 'sea'). رهای is a verbal form meaning 'to free' / 'would free' (infinitival/optative sense here), expressing the hoped-for release: 'that, if I find [it/help], [he] would free me from the fear of the sea.'
segments.s399-g41: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خواهر is the subject 'the sister'. بدانست is a past/perfective verb 'realized/understood' (literary). آواز اوی is a noun phrase 'her voice' where آواز = 'voice' and اوی is an older/genitive form meaning 'her'. The whole segment = 'when the sister realized (that) it was her voice.'
segments.s399-g42: بپوشید is a past narrative verb 'she covered/veiled' (literary form). بر خویشتن = 'over/on herself' (بر = on/upon + خویشتن = herself/oneself). راز اوی = 'her secret' (راز = secret; اوی = her). Together it means 'she wrapped/concealed her secret about herself.'
segments.s399-g43: چنان 'such/so' sets the intensity. داغ دل is a compound 'burning grief of the heart' (داغ = burning grief/wound; دل = heart). پیش او = 'in his presence / before him' (پیش = before/in front of; او = him/her). در بماند is a literary verb phrase 'remained/lingered within' (بماند = remained/linger), so the clause means 'such a grief lingered in her heart in his presence.'
segments.s399-g44: سرشک = 'a teardrop'. از دو دیده = 'from both eyes' (از = from; دو دیده = two eyes). به رخ = 'onto the cheek/face' (به = onto/towards; رخ = face/cheek). برفشاند is a past/simple poetic verb 'she scattered/spattered/let fall upon' (literary). So the clause describes a tear falling from both eyes upon the cheek.
segments.s399-g45: همه جامه چاک = 'all (her) garments torn' (همه = entirely/all; جامه = garment; چاک = torn/gashed). و دو پایش به خاک = 'and both her feet to the ground' (پای + ش = her feet; به خاک = to the ground/dust), a poetic description of being prostrate or in the dust. Word order is noun-adjective and then coordinated phrase with و.
segments.s399-g46: از ارجاسپ جانش پر از بیم و باک = 'because of Arjasp her life/soul was full of fear and dread.' از here functions causally 'because of / on account of'; ارجاسپ is the proper name Arjasp; جانش = her life/soul (جان + ش); پر from meaning 'full (of)' takes از + noun to mark the content ('پر از بیم' = 'full of fear').
segments.s399-g47: بدانست is a past/perceptive verb 'she knew / perceived'. جنگاور پاک‌رای is a nominal phrase 'a warrior of pure reputation' (جنگاور = warrior; پاک‌رای = 'of pure/clean reputation' or 'virtuous'). This is the head noun phrase being presented as known/recognized.
segments.s399-g48: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'that/whom'. او را is the object pronoun 'him' (او + را, with را marking it as definite object). همی is the literary auxiliary marking ongoing/imperative aspect in classical Persian (here indicating future/continuous sense 'would/was about to'). بازداند is the verb 'restrain/hold back' and همای is the agent 'Homay' (the bird/figure). The clause modifies جنگاور: 'whom Homay would restrain.'
segments.s399-g105: همه نامدارن (subject) + رفتند (past, 3rd person plural) + مست (result/state adjective). The order places the predicate adjective مست after the verb, a common Persian pattern meaning “they went while/being drunk.” همه modifies نامدارن ('all/the whole group of the famed').
segments.s399-g106: ز here is the poetic form of از and introduces cause or source ('from/because of'); مستی is the abstract noun 'drunkenness.' یکی functions as 'one' or 'one of them/a certain person.' شاخ نرگس is a noun phrase where شاخ ('stalk/branch') qualifies نرگس ('narcissus'). به دست is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in (his) hand' or 'held in hand' describing what that one person carried.
segments.s399-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' here. خورشید تابان is a noun (خورشید) with a postpositive adjective (تابان) — 'the radiant sun'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', گنبد is the noun 'dome'. بگشت is a past-tense verb (classical form of برگشتن) meaning 'turned/returned/turned away' and completes the temporal clause.
segments.s399-g2: خریدار بازار is a noun phrase 'buyers/customers of the market' (noun + genitive compound). او is the third-person pronoun 'him/her' referring back to a person. در گذشت is a past-tense verbal phrase 'passed (by)'; در here appears as part of the compound verbal expression and گذشت is the past verb 'passed'.
segments.s399-g3: دو خواهرش is a possessive noun phrase 'his two sisters' (دو numeral + خواهر + enclitic ش). رفتند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they went'. ز ایوان به کوی is a two-part prepositional movement 'from the iwan to the alley' using the poetic preposition ز (from) and به (to).
segments.s399-g4: This is a paired/descriptive phrase joined by و. غریوان here is a noun used as a descriptive element (literary: 'bosom/neckline' or possibly a local name) and the rest (بر کفتها بر سبوی) uses repeated بر 'on/upon' to indicate placement or contact — 'upon the kft(h)a on the ewer' (the exact referent is archaic/poetic). Grammatically, the second بر introduces a locative phrase 'on the ewer'.
segments.s399-g5: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning 'near/close to'. اسفندیار is the proper-name object of nearness. آمدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they came' and closes the clause.
segments.s399-g6: دو modifies دیده‌تر (literally 'two eye-er') — in poetic usage دیده‌تر means 'with wetter eyes' or 'tearful'; خاکسار is an adjective/noun meaning 'humble, contrite' or 'humble supplicant'. آمدند repeats the verb 'they came'. The whole phrase is a descriptive clause: 'two, tearful and contrite, came'.
segments.s399-g7: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. اسفندیار is the subject. آن شگفتی is the object phrase 'that marvel/wonder' (آن = that; شگفتی = marvel/wonder). بدید is the classical past 'saw' (ب + دید).
segments.s399-g8: دو رخ is 'two faces' (دو numeral + رخ). کرد here is the past of کردن used intransitively to indicate 'became' or 'happened' (i.e., 'were turned/occurred'). از خواهران is an ablative phrase 'from the sisters' (indicating separation or source). ناپدید is the predicate adjective/participle 'vanished/are unseen'. Altogether: 'the two faces (of the sisters) became vanished' → 'the two faces vanished.'
segments.s399-g81: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed before the verb to mark manner. ساختستم is first-person singular past (ساختم + emphatic/poetic suffix -ستم) meaning 'I made/arranged'. که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose ('so that' / 'in order that'). مهمان here is a noun used in an object-like role (مهمان کردن = 'to host, to receive as guest'), and کنم is the first-person present/subjunctive of کردن used after the noun to form the verb phrase 'to make/receive as guest' ('that I might host').
segments.s399-g82: وزین is an adjectival/adverbial modifier meaning 'weighty/dignified' ('with this weighty...') and attaches to خواهش ('request/desire'). آرایش جان is a noun phrase 'adornment (آرایش) of the soul (جان)' where جان is the possessed/affected noun. کنم repeats the first-person present/subjunctive of کردن and parallels the earlier کنم, expressing the speaker's intention ('that I may ... adorn the soul').
segments.s399-g83: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. ارجاسپ is the proper name (subject). بشنید is past tense, third-person singular (بشنود/بشنید) meaning 'he heard'. زان is an adverb 'from that/thereupon' referring back to the preceding information. شاد شد is a two-word predicate: شاد 'happy' + شد 'became' (past of شدن), so 'he became happy/was glad.'
segments.s399-g84: سر مرد نادان is a noun phrase 'the head of the foolish man' (مرد followed by its epithet نادان). پر از is an idiomatic construction meaning 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of/from'), and باد is the noun 'wind'. شد closes the clause as past 'became' (شد), so the whole line says the fool's head became full of wind (a metaphor for empty-headedness).
segments.s399-g85: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense report verb meaning 'he said' (classical/honorific form of گفت). کانکو is a proper name (the one being praised). گرامی‌ترست is the comparative adjective formed with -تر plus the archaic/persian copula -ست (short for است): 'is dearer/more honored.' Word order places the verb/predicate after the name in this poetic register.
segments.s399-g86: وزین modifies لشکر ('this weighty/dignified army')—in classical Persian such modifiers can precede the noun. امروز is a temporal adverb 'today' modifying the predicate. نامی‌ترست is another comparative ('more famed/renowned') formed with -تر + -ست (is more ...), so the clause states that the army is more famous on this day.
segments.s399-g87: به is the preposition 'to/at'. ایوان خراد is a genitive-like compound 'the iwan (hall/porch) of Khurad' (خراد is a name/abstract 'wisdom' used here as a place-name/title). مهمان شودن is the verb phrase: مهمان as noun with شدن (شوند here, third-person plural subjunctive/optative) meaning 'to be made guests' or 'to be received as guests'—an optative/polite future-like sense ('may they be guests' / 'they shall be guests').
segments.s399-g88: وگر is the conditional 'and if / but if'. می‌بود is an imperfect/conditional form of بود ('were it the case' / 'if there were'), used here to introduce a hypothetical. پاک is an adjective 'pure' (or 'completely' in adverbial use) modifying مستان ('drunkards/revelers'). شوند is again third-person plural subjunctive/optative of شدن: in the conditional clause the sense is 'they would become' or 'they would be turned into'—so the whole clause reads 'and if it were so, they'd become pure revelers.'
segments.s399-g33: که introduces a subordinate clause (here giving the content of a wish). اسفندیار is the proper name (subject). از + بنه + خود is a prepositional phrase literally 'from/of his beneh' where بنه is used poetically for 'stock/origin' (word‑order: preposition then noun then possessive خود). مباد is an archaic negative optative/jussive meaning 'may it not be' or 'let it not be', so the whole segment expresses the speaker's wish that Esfandiyar not be separated from his origin.
segments.s399-g34: نه begins a negative line ('nor' / 'not'); آن کس = 'that person/anyone' (indefinite). به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world'. کزو is a fused relative/adverbial 'from whom/from which' (ک + ز + او contracted), and کرد یاد is an older idiom where the verb کرد ('did/made') precedes the noun یاد ('remembrance') to mean 'remembered' or 'caused to be remembered'. Together: 'there is not that one in the world from whom he remembered' (i.e. 'there is no one in the world whom he remembers / from whom he took example').
segments.s399-g35: ز here functions as 'of/from' (poetic genitive/lineage marker) attaching گشتاسپ (Gushtasp) to the following noun: 'that man of Gushtasp' (i.e. a man connected with/descended from Gushtasp). آن مرد is the noun phrase 'that man', and بیدادگر is an epithet 'tyrant/cruel one' modifying مرد.
segments.s399-g36: مبیناد is an old/persianized present subjunctive/optative of نمودن/پدید آمدن meaning 'may he not appear / may he not be seen' (negative wish echoing مباد above). چون = 'like/as', او = 'he' (the person being compared), and کلاه و کمر list two items of dress ('hat and belt/girdle') — the phrase says he should not show himself attired like him. Word order places the verb-like form مبیناد before the comparison clause.
segments.s399-g37: نبینید here is an imperative/hortative negative 'do not see/consider' (addressing others) or a poetically compressed statement 'be not seen as'. کاید is used adjectivally 'crafty'/'devious' and فروشنده‌ام is 'I am a seller/merchant' with the 1st‑person clitic -ام. The construction denies a reproach: 'do not regard me as a crafty merchant.'
segments.s399-g38: ز بهر = 'for the sake of/for' (poetic order: ز then بهر). خور خویش = 'his/one's food' or 'one's sustenance' (خور here functions as 'that which is eaten / food' and خویش + ش is the possessive 'one's own'). کوشنده‌ام is a present active participle with 1st‑person copula '-ام', 'I am striving/endeavouring'. Together: 'for my own food I am striving.'
segments.s399-g39: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آواز is the direct object 'the voice/call'. بشنید is the simple past 'heard' (3rd person). فرخ همای is a nome‑appositive phrase: فرخ is an epithet 'fortunate/blessed' modifying همای (the mythical bird or a proper name), so 'the fortunate Homa heard the voice.'
segments.s399-g40: بدانست is the perfect/simple past 'she understood / recognized it' (بـ + دانست = 'knew that'). و آمد links the clauses 'and came' and combines with دلش باز جای (idiomatic sequence): آمد دلش باز جای literally 'her heart came back to its place' meaning 'her heart regained calm/returned to normal'. Note the verb آمد governs the following noun phrase as a poetic causative/result.
segments.s399-g73: یکی بزم سازم is literally 'I make a certain/one banquet.' یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' (not the numeral), بزم is 'banquet/feast', and سازم is first-person singular present/subjunctive/optative of ساختن used poetically to mean 'I will make / I shall set up.' The phrase به هر کشوری attaches a prepositional phrase 'in/into every country' (به + هر + کشوری) meaning 'in whichever country.'
segments.s399-g74: که باشد بران کشور اندر سری is a relative/subordinate clause modifying 'کشور' or qualifying the action: که ('which/that') + باشد (3rd pers. subjunctive/optative 'may be / is') introduces the uncertainty/location; بران is an older orthography of بر آن ('on/at that') pointing to that country; اندر سری means 'in secret / privately' (اندر = 'in/within', سری = 'secret'). Together: 'which may be in that country, privately there.'
segments.s399-g75: بخواهنده is 'the one who asks / the petitioner' (an agent/recipient phrase placed before the verb). بخشم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive/optative of بخشیدن meaning 'I will give / I shall bestow.' کم و بیش is an idiomatic pair 'little and much' = 'less or more / whatever amount,' and را marks that phrase as the direct object of بخشم.
segments.s399-g76: گرامی کنم is literally 'I make (someone) honored' — گرامی used adjectivally and combined with کنم (1st person 'I do/make') yields 'I honor.' مرد درویش را is the definite object ('the poor/dervish man') marked by را; درویش here reads as 'poor person / dervish.'
segments.s399-g77: کنون ... کند is a jussive/optative construction meaning 'may (he) ... now'; شاه is the subject 'the king', ما را is the object 'us' (را marks definiteness), and گرامی کند is 'may (he) honor / make honored.' The verb کند is 3rd-person subjunctive expressing a wish.
segments.s399-g78: بدین خواهش is a prepositional phrase 'by/with this request' (بدین = به + این, an archaic contraction meaning 'with this'); امروز نامی کند continues the optative sense: امروز 'today' and نامی کند 'may (he) make (us) renowned/famous' (نامی = 'famous', کند = 3rd pers. subjunctive 'may he make').
segments.s399-g79: ز لشکر is the poetic genitive/partitive 'from/of the army.' سرافراز گردان is a noun phrase 'proud / triumphant regiment(s)' (سرافراز qualifies گردان). که‌اند is a relative clause marker meaning 'which are' (که + اند, 3rd-person plural), linking these regiments to the following location phrase.
segments.s399-g80: به نزدیک شاه جهان ارجمند is a locative phrase 'near the exalted king of the world': به نزدیک = 'close to / near', شاه جهان = a grand title 'king of the world', and ارجمند = 'esteemed / noble,' an honorific adjective placed after the noun in classical/poetic word order.
segments.s399-g97: بیامد is the simple past (literary) 'came' placed before the noun for poetic emphasis. دمان ('train, hem') sits after the verb and modifies the following noun پهلوان شادکام ('the fortunate champion'), so the whole chunk reads as 'the champion came with his train' — an inverted, emphatic word order common in classical Persian.
segments.s399-g98: فراوان is an adverb ('plentifully' / 'in abundance') modifying the verb that follows. برآورد here is a literary perfective verb meaning 'set up / bring up / place (on)' (classical برآوردن), followed by its object هیزم ('firewood') and the prepositional phrase به بام ('onto the roof'). The verb is transitive and the phrase as a whole means 'he brought/heaped much firewood onto the roof.'
segments.s399-g99: بکشتند is a past plural verb 'they killed/slaughtered' placed before its objects (common in epic diction). اسپان (horses) and چندی بره ('several/some lambs') are direct objects coordinated by و; چندی quantifies بره. No object marker is needed in Persian.
segments.s399-g100: کشیدند (past plural of کشیدن) here means 'they hauled/dragged/carried' and is followed by the prepositional phrase بر بام ('onto the roof') plus دژ ('fortress') specifying whose roof, with یکسره ('completely/entirely') as an adverbial qualifier. The verb governs the whole directional phrase: 'they hauled (them) up onto the fortress's roof completely.'
segments.s399-g101: ز is the poetic/genitive preposition 'from' ('because of' in some contexts). هیزم is the noun 'firewood'; که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. بر باره دژ combines بر ('on/upon') with باره in the sense 'load/burden' — here 'the load placed on the fortress' — and کشید is the verb 'drew/placed' (the usual sense would be 'کشیدند' but here the form/lemma choices in the list are irregular); the whole segment means 'from the firewood which they drew up on the fortress.'
segments.s399-g102: شد is the simple past/auxiliary 'became' or 'came to be' and here introduces the resulting state. از دود ('from smoke') is an ablative phrase, روی هوا ('into/on the face of the air' = 'into the air/sky') is a standard poetic locution, and ناپدید is an adjective meaning 'vanished/invisible'. Together: 'it became (or was) vanished in the air from smoke' — i.e., smoke rose and the place was lost sight of in the air.
segments.s399-g103: می‌آورد is an imperfective/habitual present used narratively ('he would bring' or 'he was bringing'). چون here functions temporally 'when/whenever' (classical usage), introducing the subordinate clause. هرچ ('whatever/anything') is the object, بد خورده شد is a passive/perfect construction 'was eaten poorly' or 'had been badly consumed/handled' — خورده (past participle) + شد (become/auxiliary) gives a passive sense.
segments.s399-g104: گسارنده is an agent noun 'the rending/breaking/devourer' (a poetic epithet). می‌ورا is a rare/poetic verb form 'devours' (presentive), and برده شد is a passive perfect 'was carried/taken away' ('برده' = 'taken' + شد). Grammatically, the sequence attributes the action to the devourer: whatever had been badly eaten, the devourer took away.
segments.s399-g25: برهنه دوان is an adjectival + participial-like sequence: برهنه (naked) + دوان (running/rushing), giving 'running naked'. بر سر انجمن is a prepositional phrase ('بر' + noun) meaning 'on/upon/into the middle of the assembly' (سر انجمن = the head/center of the gathering). The order is typical of classical Persian where descriptive phrase precedes the locative.
segments.s399-g26: خنک here is a predicate adjective placed before a relative clause and carries an idiomatic sense of 'pleasant/fortunate' (literally 'cool'). آنک is the classical relative 'that/who'; پوشد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive used narratively ('he covers' / 'who covers'); تنش را shows the possessed noun (تن + enclitic ش = 'his body') followed by the object marker را; کفن is the object of پوشد ('a shroud').
segments.s399-g27: بگرییم is 1st-person plural present/subjunctive used hortatively ('let us weep'); چندی is an adverb 'for a while'. به + noun here functions instrumentally ('with'), so به خونین سرشک = 'with bloody teardrops' (سرشک = teardrop).
segments.s399-g28: تو باشی is a jussive/optative 2nd-person form 'may you be' or 'be you'. بدین is the classical compound of به + این ('to/for this'); the tokenization shows ب + دین — دین here represents the demonstrative 'این' (an orthographic/metrical form). درد ما را پزشک is a syntactic unit meaning 'our physician for this pain' where ما را marks the affected party ('for us'/'our') and پزشک is the predicative noun. Overall: 'May you be our physician for this pain.'
segments.s399-g29: گر is the conditional 'if'. آگاهیت is a noun with 2nd-person possessive suffix (-ت) meaning 'your knowledge/awareness'; هست is the copula 'is/exists'. از شهر ما literally 'of/from our city'—classical Persian often uses 'هست' after the possessed noun: 'if your knowledge is of our city' = 'if you know our city.'
segments.s399-g30: برین بوم = بر + ین + بوم, a classical phrasing meaning 'on this soil/land' (ین = orthographic/poetic variant of این 'this'). تریاک شد is Subject + past verb 'became' (تریاک became ...). زهر ما is the predicate 'our poison' (زهر = poison, ما = our/us); the verb شد places 'became' before the predicate, a common classical order: 'opium became our poison.'
segments.s399-g31: یکی is an indefinite 'someone'/'a certain one'. بانگ برزد is an idiomatic classical verbal phrase: بانگ (a cry/sound) + برزد (an archaic verb meaning 'arose/was sounded' — here rendered 'a cry sounded'). به زیر گلیم is a locative phrase 'beneath/under the rug' (به indicating direction/location + زیر = under).
segments.s399-g32: که introduces the result/clause 'so that/and then'. لرزان شد ند is a compound predicate: لرزان (trembling) + شدند (became, past plural) = 'they became trembling'. آن ... دختر is the subject 'those/that (two) maidens'; ز بیم is an ablative/explanatory phrase 'from/with fear' giving the cause. Note the classical order with the verb before the explicit subject.
segments.s399-g49: سبک روی بگشاد is a compact pictorial clause: سبک روی = 'a light/gentle face' (adjective + noun), بگشاد is the past tense of گشودن 'to open/lift' (here 'he lifted' or 'unfolded' his face). The conjunction و then links to the next nominal phrase about the eyes; word order (adjective+noun + verb) is typical poetic Persian.
segments.s399-g50: پر از خون دل is a genitive phrase: پر (full) + از (of/from) + خون دل ('blood of the heart', an idiom for inner bleeding/sorrow). Then و introduces چهره چون آفتاب, a simile 'a countenance like the sun.' The sequence strings descriptive nominal phrases after the initial verb, a common epic style.
segments.s399-g51: ز کار جهان ماند اندر شگفت: ز کار جهان = 'at/from the affairs of the world' (ز + noun phrase), ماند is the past of ماندن 'to remain/be left', and اندر شگفت = 'in wonder/amazement'. The prepositional/poetic ordering (ز ... ماند اندر ...) places cause ('the world's affairs') before the result ('was left in wonder').
segments.s399-g52: دژم گشت = 'became sullen' (دژم adjective + گشت 'became/turned'). لب را به دندان گرفت is an idiomatic object construction: لب (lip) marked by را as definite object, به دندان 'to/against the tooth', گرفت past 'took' — together meaning 'he took his lip to his teeth' i.e. 'bit his lip.'
segments.s399-g53: بدیشان چنین گفت = 'he said to them thus' (بدیشان = to/for them; چنین = thus; گفت is the past verb 'said'). کاین روز چند is a rhetorical question: کاین = 'that/this', روز = 'day', چند = 'how long/how much' → 'how long will this day (last/continue)?' Note: گفت is the quoted-speech verb introducing the direct speech.
segments.s399-g54: بدارید هر دو لبان را به بند is an imperative: بدارید (2nd-person plural imperative/command of داشتن 'keep/hold'), هر دو لبان را = 'both lips' with را marking the definite object, and به بند = 'under/to a bond' (بند here = 'fetter/bond') — idiomatically 'keep your lips bound' = 'hold your tongues/keep silent.'
segments.s399-g55: من ایدر نه از بهر جنگ آمدم: من = 'I', ایدر = 'here', نه is the negation 'not' (appears in the line though not listed among the word items), از بهر = an archaic compound meaning 'for the sake of' or 'on account of', جنگ = 'war', آمدم = past 'I came'. The clause therefore negates the purpose: 'I did not come here for war.'
segments.s399-g56: به رنج از پی نام و ننگ آمدم: به رنج = 'to/into hardship' or 'through suffering' (به + noun), از پی = 'in pursuit of' (پی after از denotes following/pursuit), نام = 'name/reputation', و = 'and', ننگ here used in classical epic sense as 'renown/glory' (though literally 'shame' in modern use), آمدم = 'I came'. The whole clause explains his motive: 'I came through suffering in pursuit of fame and glory.'
segments.s399-g89: 'بدو گفت' is the common phrasing 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', followed by the past verb گفت = 'said'). 'شاها' is a vocative 'O king' addressing the addressee. 'ردا' is a noun 'cloak/robe' used metaphorically ('a cloak to the wise'), and 'بخردا' here means 'to the wise' (ب- + خردا) forming a genitive-like phrase; word order places the vocative and then the predicate noun phrase.
segments.s399-g90: 'جهاندار' is an appositive title 'sovereign/ruler'. The conjunction 'و' links this title to the next phrase. 'بر' is the preposition 'over/upon' marking authority, 'موبدان' is the plural 'mobeds (Zoroastrian priests)', and 'موبدا' is a title 'mobeda' (chief priest) used here as a further appositive; thus the phrase reads 'and (you are) a mobeda over the mobeds.'
segments.s399-g91: مرا خانه تنگست literally 'to me the house is narrow' — 'مرا' marks possession ('my' / 'to me'), 'خانه' is the noun 'house', and 'تنگست' is the copula+adjective 'is cramped/narrow'. The conjunction 'و' introduces the contrasting noun phrase 'کاخ بلند' ('a lofty palace').
segments.s399-g92: 'برین باره' = 'on this occasion' (برین = 'this'/'upon this', ین = orthographic/DEM element, باره = 'occasion/instance'). 'دژ شویم' uses شویم (1st-person plural subjunctive/optative) meaning 'may we become' or 'let us become', and 'ارجمند' is the predicative adjective 'honored/esteemed' modifying 'دژ' ('fortress'), so the clause expresses a wish: 'may we become an honored fortress on this occasion.'
segments.s399-g93: در مهر ماه = 'in the month of Mehr' (در + the month-name + ماه). آمد ... کنم is a purposive construction: آمد (past 'came') + a following verb with کنم (subjunctive/infinitival use of کردن) expresses 'came in order to ...'; آتش کنم therefore means 'to kindle/make a fire'. The subject 'I' is omitted (typical in Persian poetry).
segments.s399-g94: دل نامداران = 'the hearts of the renowned' (noun + noun genitive construction common in classical Persian; ezāfe is often omitted in verse). به می‌خوش کنم = 'to make (them) merry with wine': به introduces the instrument/means ('with' wine), می‌خوش is a lexicalized verbal/adjectival stem meaning 'made happy/joyful (by wine)', and کنم repeats the purposive/subjunctive 'to do' tying this clause to the previous آمد.
segments.s399-g95: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو is the older/prepositional form 'to him/unto him', followed by گفت 'said'). زان راه روکت هواست is a verbless/equational clause: زان راه = 'that way', روکت is a nominalized verbal idea ('going/your going' or 'the going by that way'), and هواست = هَوَا ('desire') + ست (copula) = 'is desire' → 'that way is what you desire/ wish to go'.
segments.s399-g96: به کاخ اندرون = 'to/into the inner palace' (به used locatively, اندرون = 'inside/inner quarters'). میزبان پادشاست = predicate nominative: 'the host is the king' (میزبان = host; پادشاست = پادشا/king + ست copula 'is'). Word order places the locative phrase first, then the nominal sentence 'میزبان پادشاست.'
segments.s399-g17: This is an optative clause introduced by که + ... + باد: که ... فرخنده باد = "may ... be fortunate/auspicious." روز و شبان is a poetic pairing "day and night" (شبان is an archaic/poetic form here). فرخنده is the predicative adjective "auspicious/fortunate," and باد is the optative particle "may it be." Word order: temporal phrase (روز و شبان) + preposition بر ("upon") + target تو + predicate فرخنده + optative باد.
segments.s399-g18: همه مهتران = "all the chiefs/elders/great ones" (مهتران = plural of a word meaning 'senior/great one'); پیش تو = "before you / in your presence / to you." بنده باد is again an optative formula: بنده (servant) used predicatively and باد = "may it be," i.e. "may they be your servants."
segments.s399-g19: A simple prepositional list introduced by ز (poetic fromِ) meaning "from / concerning": ز ایران و گشتاسپ و اسفندیار = "about/from Iran and Gushtasp and Esfandiyar." The proper names follow the preposition in a coordinated list.
segments.s399-g20: چه آگاهی است = "what information/news is there?" چه = interrogative "what" and آگاهی = "information/news." است‌ای is est + the vocative clitic ای (here written attached) — a common poetic shape meaning roughly "is, O..." گو is an imperative "tell/say" (addressing the speaker), and نامدار is a vocative epithet "renowned/illustrious." The line therefore asks for news and addresses the speaker to tell it.
segments.s399-g21: بدین سان is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning "in this way / thus." دو دخت = "two maidens/daughters." یکی پادشا here means "one (of them) (is) a king's [daughter] / one is royal" (یکی marks one of the two as the royal one); the copula is omitted as is normal in older Persian.
segments.s399-g22: اسیریم is the first-person plural present/passive-like form "we are captives / we are taken (captive)." در دست = an idiom meaning "in the power/hands of," where دست means "power/control." ناپارسا is an adjective "impious / ungodly / wicked," so در دست ناپارسا = "in the hands of the impious."
segments.s399-g23: A sequence of descriptive phrases: برهنه modifies the following body parts (سر و پای) meaning "bare/naked" (i.e. without covering). دوش here means "on the shoulder" (دوش = shoulder), so دوش آبکش = "with a colander (آبکش) on the shoulder." Word order is additive: "bare-headed and bare-footed, and on the shoulder a colander."
segments.s399-g24: پدر شادمان = "the father, joyful" (شادمان either as adjective after the noun or a nominal phrase). روز و شب gives the time-frame "day and night," and خفته is a past-participial adjective "asleep" used predicatively (no copula). خوش here functions adverbially "happily/well." The phrase means "the father, rejoicing, sleeps happily day and night."

sentences.s399-s29: Anyone whose daughter was a sieve — the son in sorrow, and the father in pleasant sleep.
sentences.s399-s30: May the father be the sky and the mother the earth; I will not shout 'bravo' upon these times.
sentences.s399-s31: Then the young man rose from the cottage and came running toward Arjasp.
sentences.s399-s32: He said to him, 'O king, be fortunate; sovereign, live forever.'
sentences.s399-s5: His heart became full of fear at their deed; he hid his face beneath the kilim.
sentences.s399-s6: Both of them went up close to him; their faces, smeared with blood, were turned toward the two streams.
sentences.s399-s7: At the pleading, the helpless seized that famed merchant.
sentences.s399-s8: The sister said to him, 'O caravan‑leader, from where did you first drive the caravan?'
sentences.s399-s33: Along this route there was a deep sea, of which the merchants were not aware.
sentences.s399-s34: From the sea there arose a certain crooked wind, which the helmsman said, 'I do not recall that.'
sentences.s399-s35: On the ship we all became wretched and weeping; from our very life and body we were scorched.
sentences.s399-s36: I appealed to the just one, the one God, asking that if I should obtain [help], he would free me from the fear of the sea.
sentences.s399-s21: When the sister realized it was her voice, she concealed her secret within herself.
sentences.s399-s22: Such a burning grief remained in her heart before him that she let a teardrop fall from both eyes onto her cheek.
sentences.s399-s23: All her garments were torn and her two feet were on the ground; because of Arjasp her soul was full of fear and dread.
sentences.s399-s24: She perceived the noble (pure-reputed) warrior whom Homay would restrain.
sentences.s399-s53: All the renowned went off drunk; in their drunkenness one held a narcissus stalk in his hand.
sentences.s399-s1: When the radiant sun turned away from the dome, the market's buyers passed him by.
sentences.s399-s2: His two sisters left the iwan and went into the lane, with their bosoms (or scarves) — and with jugs upon (their) ewers.
sentences.s399-s3: They came near Esfandiyar; tearful and contrite, they came.
sentences.s399-s4: When Esfandiyar saw that marvel, the two faces of the sisters vanished.
sentences.s399-s41: I fashioned it thus so that I might entertain (him) as a guest, and by this weighty request adorn the soul.
sentences.s399-s42: When Arjasp heard that, he became glad; the fool's head was filled with wind.
sentences.s399-s43: He said, 'Kan̄ku is more honored, and this army today is more renowned.'
sentences.s399-s44: They will be guests in the Hall of Khurad, and if it were so, they would become pure revelers.
sentences.s399-s17: May Esfandiyar not be estranged from his own stock; there is no one in the world from whom he took remembrance.
sentences.s399-s18: May that cruel man of Gushtasp not appear like him in hat and belt.
sentences.s399-s19: Do not take me for a crafty seller; I strive for my own food.
sentences.s399-s20: When the fortunate Homa heard the voice, she recognized it and her heart returned to its place.
sentences.s399-s37: I will set up a feast in whatever country it may be, keeping it secret there in that land.
sentences.s399-s38: To whoever asks I will give little or much; I will honor the poor man.
sentences.s399-s39: May our king now honor us; by this request may he make us famed today.
sentences.s399-s40: From the army's proud regiments, which are near the noble king of the world.
sentences.s399-s49: The fortunate champion arrived in his flowing train, and he heaped plentiful firewood on the roof.
sentences.s399-s50: They slaughtered the horses and a number of lambs, and dragged them up onto the roof of the fortress entirely.
sentences.s399-s51: From the firewood that they had piled on the fortress came smoke, and (the fortress) was lost in the air.
sentences.s399-s52: He would bring (it); whenever anything had been eaten poorly, the devourer would carry it off.
sentences.s399-s13: Running naked into the midst of the assembly, fortunate is he who wraps his body in a shroud.
sentences.s399-s14: Let us weep for a while with bloody teardrops; may you be the physician to our pain.
sentences.s399-s15: If you have knowledge of our city, on this soil our opium has become poison for us.
sentences.s399-s16: Someone uttered a cry beneath the rug; the two maidens trembled with fear.
sentences.s399-s25: He lifted his gentle face; his eyes were brimming with tears, his heart bleeding, and his countenance like the sun.
sentences.s399-s26: He was left amazed at the affairs of the world; he became sullen and bit his lip.
sentences.s399-s27: He said to them thus: 'How long will you endure this day? Keep both your lips bound.'
sentences.s399-s28: I did not come here for the sake of battle; I came through hardship in pursuit of fame and glory.
sentences.s399-s45: He said to him, “O king, you are a cloak to the wise, a sovereign, and the mobeda over the mobeds.”
sentences.s399-s46: My house is cramped and the palace is lofty; on this occasion may we become an honored fortress.
sentences.s399-s47: In the month of Mehr I came to kindle a fire and to make the hearts of the renowned merry with wine.
sentences.s399-s48: He said to him: 'That way is what you wish; within the palace the host is the king.'
sentences.s399-s9: May day and night be auspicious for you; may all the chiefs be your servants.
sentences.s399-s10: From Iran and Gushtasp and Esfandiyar, what news is there? Tell us, O renowned one.
sentences.s399-s11: Thus — two maidens, one (a king's daughter) — we are captives in the hands of the impious.
sentences.s399-s12: Bareheaded and barefoot, and with a colander on her shoulder; the father, rejoicing, sleeps happily day and night.
